cards.back: Deliberately ignoring irrelevant detail so you can focus on what matters for the problem at hand.
Splitting a large problem into smaller, more manageable sub-problems that can be solved (and tested) independently.
An abstract model that is always in exactly one of a finite number of states, and moves between states by reading input symbols one at a time.
A state (drawn with a double circle) such that, if the FSM ends there after reading the whole input, the input is accepted.
The set of all strings that some finite state machine accepts — exactly what a simple regular expression can describe.
A theoretical model of computation: an infinite tape, a head that reads/writes/moves one cell at a time, a finite set of states, and a table of transition rules.
The problem of deciding, for an arbitrary program and input, whether the program will eventually halt. Proved undecidable by Alan Turing.
A proof technique that builds an object specifically designed to contradict any answer a hypothetical solver could give — used to prove the halting problem undecidable.
A problem for which some algorithm exists that always terminates and always gives the correct yes/no answer.
A problem for which no algorithm can exist that always terminates with a correct answer, no matter how much time or memory is allowed — e.g. the halting problem.
A problem solvable in polynomial time (running time bounded by a fixed power of the input size, e.g. $n^2$) — stays practical as the input grows.
A problem for which every known algorithm needs exponential (or worse) time, e.g. $2^n$ — quickly becomes infeasible even for modest input sizes.
